museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Track & Field reaches arcades, Texas Instruments prepares to leave the home-computer market, and the UK micro press turns autumn into a buying guide for a crowded Christmas.
museum.curatorNote: October's strongest exact anchors are market and arcade-context rather than a single dominant UK release. Some late-1983 Spectrum titles are included with month-level caution.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: October is sport, retreat and seasonal pressure.

items.title: Track & Field reaches arcades
items.body: Konami's button-battering sports cabinet turned physical performance into public play and helped define a new competitive arcade rhythm.

items.title: Texas Instruments moves toward withdrawal
items.body: After brutal price competition, TI's home-computer story was nearing its end, with production and market exit reported in the autumn.
items.sourceNote: TI-99/4A histories place withdrawal/discontinuation decisions in late 1983.

items.title: Electron User begins publication
items.body: The new Acorn Electron had enough gravity to support its own UK magazine, even as supply problems shadowed the machine.
items.certainty: verified month by magazine archive summaries
items.sourceNote: Electron User references list first issue in October 1983.

items.title: Christmas computer buying begins early
items.body: Families weighing Spectrum, C64, BBC, Electron and Dragon were now reading specifications as holiday decisions.

items.title: Console crash coverage becomes part of the backdrop
items.body: By October, the North American crash was a business reality, not just a warning sign, but games culture kept moving through computers and arcades.
items.sourceNote: Crash histories and autumn 1983 market context.

items.title: Track & Field
items.body: A competitive sports cabinet built around timed events and frantic inputs.

items.title: Ant Attack
items.body: Quicksilva's isometric survival game belongs to late-1983 Spectrum history and is often remembered as a 3D landmark.
items.sourceNote: Included as late-1983 context, not a firm October claim.

items.title: Manic Miner
items.body: Still central to the Spectrum's autumn identity.

items.title: Lode Runner
items.body: Its level editor and puzzle structure kept it alive beyond launch month.

items.title: Ultima III: Exodus
items.body: A serious computer RPG still inviting maps, parties and long evenings.

items.title: Famicom launch library
items.body: Nintendo's early Japanese console catalogue was still small but important.

items.title: Star Wars
items.body: Still a high-prestige cabinet and a reminder that Atari's arcade craft survived Atari's market problems.

items.title: Acorn Electron software promise
items.body: The new machine's software identity was still forming as buyers looked toward Christmas.
items.sourceNote: Electron launched August 1983; supply and software context.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: October's hardware case is about fatigue and new hope.

items.title: TI-99/4A
items.body: A home computer with real technical interest, damaged by a price war it could not sustain.

items.title: Acorn Electron
items.body: A promising UK home micro whose demand and supply did not align cleanly before Christmas.

items.title: Konami Track & Field cabinet
items.body: A cabinet that made control wear and physical effort part of the game culture.

items.title: ZX Spectrum 48K
items.body: The Spectrum remained the affordable UK games machine around which magazines and schoolyard talk orbited.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: October magazines were partly buying guides and partly survival manuals.

items.title: Electron User
items.headline: A new Acorn machine gets its own shelf voice
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electron User style cover for October 1983.
items.curatorNote: The Electron's own magazine presence shows how quickly UK micros became communities.
items.body: The Electron's own magazine presence shows how quickly UK micros became communities.
items.sourceNote: Electron User issue history lists October 1983 start.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Autumn arcade sport and micro choices
items.curatorNote: Track & Field-style arcade energy sat beside the practical question of which micro to buy.
items.body: Track & Field-style arcade energy sat beside the practical question of which micro to buy.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: The autumn home-computer shelf
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for October 1983.
items.curatorNote: A serious computer magazine could legitimise a games purchase as household technology.
items.body: A serious computer magazine could legitimise a games purchase as household technology.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum games before Christmas
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for October 1983.
items.curatorNote: Spectrum owners were no longer waiting for a scene. They were inside one.
items.body: Spectrum owners were no longer waiting for a scene. They were inside one.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: October's network was seasonal and practical.

items.title: Buying guides became important
items.body: Families needed help choosing between too many machines.

items.title: Arcade techniques spread by imitation
items.body: Track & Field had a physical lore of tapping methods and sore fingers.

items.title: Machine communities formed quickly
items.body: A new micro could gain a magazine, listings and identity almost at once.

items.title: Crash knowledge mixed with bargains
items.body: Bad business news could still mean cheap games on a shelf.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: October felt like choosing carefully under bright shop lights.

items.title: The arcade became athletic
items.body: Track & Field made competition noisy and physical.

items.title: Some machines were already cautionary tales
items.body: TI showed how quickly a home-computer promise could turn.

items.title: The Electron felt hopeful and awkward
items.body: A desirable British machine, but not always easy to actually get.

items.title: The Spectrum felt established
items.body: Its games culture had become ordinary in the best sense.

sources.label: Track & Field context
sources.note: Used for October 1983 arcade release context.

sources.label: Electron User context
sources.note: Used for October 1983 first issue context.

sources.label: Ant Attack context
sources.note: Used for 1983 Spectrum release context; exact month marked uncertain.
